main_summary.summary: William Saunders (born New York City, 1935; raised on Johns Island, South Carolina) recounts a six-decade trajectory from Sea Island farm childhood to lead organizer of the 1969 Charleston hospital workers' strike. Sent south at eighteen months, raised by grandparents in a Gullah farming community, he came to political consciousness through Esau Jenkins, the Progressive Club, and the District 9 school-board struggle. After volunteering for the U.S. Army at sixteen, he served in the Korean War, was wounded by a booby-trap mine on December 26, 1951, and returned home embittered by Army segregation and the Greyhound station encounter in Columbia. He attended a 1959 SCLC-Highlander Research Center session in Monteagle, Tennessee and was present during the raid that arrested Septima Clark. He rejected the nonviolent stance, identifying with Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X while continuing to provide support work — paperwork, food, mimeographed newsletters — through the Progressive Club. From his mattress-factory job he ran the Low Country Newsletter (built around equipment from Dr. Howard Levy and the American Friends Service Committee), helped organize the 1969 Medical College Hospital strike alongside Otis Robinson, and conducted back-channel negotiations with vice-president William Huffitz that brought trustees and worker leadership together. He details Governor Robert E. McNair's attempt to use him to deceive striking workers, SLED Chief J.P. "Pete" Strom's warning of an assassination plot, and Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s role in cutting HEW funding to force resolution. The interview also covers his 1980 state-senate run, NAACP opposition, and founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program.
key_themes: Korean War service and racism in the U.S. military
class analysis of the civil rights movement
main_summary.historical_significance:
Saunders's testimony provides a first-person account of the 1969 Charleston hospital workers' strike from inside the local organizing apparatus — distinct from the SCLC and 1199 union narratives that have dominated the historiography. He claims the slogan 'I AM SOMEBODY' originated with local Black Muslim leader Otis Robinson at a 200-person worker meeting on the night of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, not with Jesse Jackson's later PUSH-era popularization. He documents back-channel negotiations through Medical College vice-president William Huffitz, Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s intervention with the Nixon Administration to cut HEW funding to the hospital, and Governor Robert E. McNair's attempted deception that would have used Saunders to falsely announce a settlement to congregations at Morris Brown AME and Emanuel AME.

The interview also documents a Sea Islands organizing lineage running through Esau Jenkins, the Progressive Club, the 1959 Highlander raid that arrested Septima Clark, and Saunders's own rejection of nonviolence as a philosophy. His class-rather-than-race framework — formed through the Highlander encounter with poor Appalachian whites — and his account of NAACP opposition to his 1980 state-senate candidacy in favor of I. DeQuincey Newman supplies a counter-narrative to the conventional civil rights leadership account.

chapters.title: Johns Island Childhood
chapters.summary: Saunders describes being born in New York City in 1935, sent at eighteen months to Johns Island, South Carolina to be raised by his maternal grandparents, and growing up in a predominantly African American farming community. He recounts his grandfather as a self-taught land surveyor, the working day of 'can to can't,' selling chickens and lightwood at the Charleston market, and his first trip to Charleston after being burned standing too close to a roadside fire.

chapters.title: Meeting Esau Jenkins
chapters.summary: Saunders recalls meeting Esau Jenkins through church and school as a young child and watching him build a Sea Islands political base. He describes the District 9 school board's freeze on elections from 1956 to 1970, the appointment system used to keep Black candidates off, and the 1967-1968 organizing push (alongside Stokely Carmichael's visits to Johns Island) that forced the board to appoint Jenkins and one other Black member.

chapters.title: Korean War Service
chapters.summary: At sixteen, ostracized by Charleston schoolmates at Burke High, Saunders volunteered for the U.S. Army with classmates and was sent through Fort Jackson, Fort Lewis, Hawaii, and into Korean War combat by LST landing. He describes more racism in the integrated Army than he had encountered in South Carolina, an incident in which his first sergeant pointed a .45 at him on the front line, and being wounded by a booby-trap trip-flare mine on December 26, 1951.

chapters.title: Greyhound Station Awakening
chapters.summary: Returning from Korean War wounds with $500 of back pay, Saunders walked into the front of the Columbia, South Carolina Greyhound bus station and was confronted at gunpoint by a police officer who directed him to the segregated Black waiting area outside. He describes this moment as the start of his political bitterness, the realization that his white Army friends had never advocated for him, and his return to the Progressive Club and Esau Jenkins.

chapters.title: Highlander 1959 Raid
chapters.summary: Saunders attended a 1959 Highlander Research Center session in Monteagle, Tennessee, and was present during the raid that arrested Septima Clark on a beer-selling pretext and led to the seizure of Highlander's property. He describes refusing to hand over his car keys until Esau Jenkins and I. DeQuincey Newman intervened, encountering Myles Horton and poor Appalachian whites at Highlander, and beginning to view American inequality as a class system rather than a race system.

chapters.title: Rejecting Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Saunders explains his break with SCLC and Esau Jenkins's nonviolent strategy after the Highlander raid and his time in Harlem, where he sought out the writings of Malcolm X and Stokely Carmichael. He continued to do support work — paperwork, hosting Fannie Lou Hamer and other Movement organizers on Johns Island, providing food at the Progressive Club — but refused to march or be beaten. He recounts Fannie Lou Hamer's protective profanity tactic and his philosophical reading of self-defense.

chapters.title: Mattress Factory and Newsletter
chapters.summary: Saunders graduated from Burke High School in 1956 and went to work in a Charleston mattress factory under owner Julius Walsh, who tolerated his organizing and installed a private telephone at his sewing machine. He launched the Low Country Newsletter using equipment given by Dr. Howard Levy (a U.S. Army dermatologist who refused Vietnam deployment) and printing press support from the American Friends Service Committee, with early issues run as mimeographs through a sympathetic Navy Yard contact. The newsletter targeted District 9, Johns Island, and Wadmalaw.

chapters.title: Against School Integration
chapters.summary: Saunders details Charleston's consolidated school board scheme that placed an at-large body atop the existing constituent boards and stripped local power, the NAACP's focus on placing 'certain colored kids' in schools like ICS and Avery Normal Institute, and his own opposition to busing. He recounts winning election to the Office of Economic Opportunity board in 1967, the NAACP's petition to the governor not to certify him, and his confrontational reading of Millicent Brown's family's role in Charleston school desegregation cases.

chapters.title: Organizing the Hospital Workers
chapters.summary: Saunders describes the 1968 origin of the Medical College Hospital workers' organizing committee, beginning when five Black nurses were fired and reinstated through Reginald Barrett of the U.S. Civil Rights Commission. He recounts the cell-doubling recruitment method (six bringing six, twelve bringing twelve) until meetings moved from Barrett's house to the DPO Hall and the Cigar Factory, reaching roughly three hundred organized workers. He explains the workers' demands: minimum wage, job descriptions, dignity, and an end to segregated boiler-room lunch arrangements.

chapters.title: 'I AM SOMEBODY' Origin
chapters.summary: Saunders attributes the slogan 'I AM SOMEBODY' to Otis Robinson, leader of Charleston's Black Muslim community, who spoke to approximately 200 hospital workers on the night Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated. After the hospital president had publicly disparaged the workers as unimportant, Robinson told them they were with patients twenty-four hours a day, cleaning and feeding them — that they were somebody. Saunders is explicit that Jesse Jackson later took the phrase but that it originated with Robinson at this specific Charleston meeting.

chapters.title: SCLC and 1199 Arrive
chapters.summary: Saunders describes the January 1969 entry of Local 1199 and SCLC into the Charleston strike, working alongside Rev. Andrew Young. He notes that the workers were already organized when the union and SCLC arrived. He recounts his disagreement with the union and SCLC over their refusal to train hospital workers in administrative skills, and his recruitment of young Black men with prior records to serve as protective guards and as voluntary jail-fillers who would protect arrested workers from inside the cells.

chapters.title: Back-Channel Negotiations
chapters.summary: Saunders details his role conducting back-channel negotiations during the strike. He met with Medical College vice-president William Huffitz during the curfew, exchanged information about trustee and union positions, and brokered a Moe's Corners meeting where the two sides resolved eight of ten worker demands. He describes a SLED Chief J.P. "Pete" Strom warning of an assassination plot against him, and his own armed self-defense apparatus that he characterizes as 'offensive' rather than defensive.

chapters.title: Governor McNair's Setup
chapters.summary: Saunders describes a phone call from Governor Robert E. McNair asking him to address Morris Brown AME and Emanuel AME congregations that night and promise the strike would end the next morning. Suspicious, Saunders asked for a written telegram; McNair refused; Saunders went home instead. About 200 people were arrested at the church that night. He explains that McNair was attempting to use his credibility to spread a lie that would have turned the workers against him. He also details Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s call to the Nixon Administration urging HEW to cut $75 million in funding to force settlement.

chapters.title: Strike Settlement and Aftermath
chapters.summary: Saunders reflects on the strike's settlement, noting his bitterness that no local Charleston organizer was credited — the public account credited SCLC and 1199 while Rev. Andrew Young's national career took off. He recounts the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission's founding out of the strike, the subsequent election of six Black members to Charleston City Council, House of Representatives seats, and County Council seats, and the immediate roll-forward into the Charleston sanitation strike that won minimum wage and equipment access for sanitation workers.
related_events: South Carolina Human Affairs Commission founding

chapters.title: 1980 State Senate Run
chapters.summary: Saunders describes his 1980 bid for the South Carolina State Senate, winning the Democratic primary and runoff, then losing the general election in what he attributes to Ronald Reagan's first-year coattail effect and NAACP opposition. He claims the NAACP, working with figures who brought people in from Atlanta, opposed him because they had already arranged for I. DeQuincey Newman to become the first Black state senator through a planned special election to follow a sitting senator's resignation for a judgeship.

chapters.title: Radio and Public Service
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts his work running a Charleston radio station that won number-one ratings in a 27-station market through community-service programming, gospel, talk, and jazz. He describes pre-Reagan FCC rules that required public-service content before commercial sales, the lawsuit attempts to take his station down, and listeners who removed station-knobs from family radios. He laments the deregulation that followed.

chapters.title: Gullah and Sickle Cell
chapters.summary: Saunders describes his early shame of Gullah language ('Gullah was such a bad word') and the 1969-1970 South Carolina legislation that recognized Gullah as a language. He recounts founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program in 1970, its current 15-16 percent sickle-cell trait carrier rate in the local population, a recent case of a four-year-old boy who had a stroke, and the 75 percent budget cut that has imperiled the program.
keywords: Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program
related_events: Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program founding 1970

chapters.title: Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Saunders closes with concerns about Sea Islands development pressure, the purchase of three or four county-council politicians to override five citizen-vote losses on a road-across-the-island, and the difference between leaders and managers. He describes his concerned-citizen organizing model on Johns Island that brought together former Klansmen, farmers, and Movement veterans by refusing to discuss religion or politics and focusing only on whether issues were right or wrong. He says his proudest accomplishment is reaching age 76 without being indicted by his government.

loc_video.caption: William Saunders oral history interview conducted by Kieran Walsh Taylor in Charleston, South Carolina, 2011 June 09
